title: Metabolism of peptide amino acids by Chinese hamster ovary cells grown in a complex medium
abstract:
Metabolism of Peptide Amino Acids by
Chinese Hamster Ovary Cells Grown in a
Complex Medium
Gregg B. Nyberg,* R. Robert Balcarcel, Brian D. Follstad,
Gregory Stephanopoulos, Daniel I. C. Wang
Department of Chemical Engineering, Biotechnology Process Engineering
Center, Room 56-469C, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, MIT, 77
Massachusetts Avenue, Cambridge, Massachusetts 02139; telephone:
617-253-4583; fax: 617-253-3122; e-mail: gregstep@mit.edu
Received 22 January 1998; accepted 31 July 1998
Abstract: Metabolic flux analysis is a useful tool for un-
raveling relationships between metabolism and cell
function. Material balancing can be used to provide es-
timates of major metabolic pathway fluxes, provided all
significant metabolite uptake and production rates are
measured. Potential sources of metabolizable material in
many serum-free media formulations are low molecular
weight digests of biological material such as yeast ex-
tracts and plant or animal tissue hydrolysates. These di-
gests typically contain large amounts of peptides, which
may be utilized as amino acids. This article demonstrates
the need for accounting for amino acids liberated from
peptides in order to accurately estimate pathway fluxes
in Chinese hamster ovary cells grown in a complex (hy-
drolysate containing) medium. A simplified model of
central carbon metabolism provides the framework for
analyzing external metabolite measurements. Redun-
dant measurements are included to ensure the consis-
tency of data and assumed biochemistry by comparing
redundant measurements with their predicted values
from a minimum data set, and by expressing the degree
of agreement using a statistical ‘‘consistency index.’’ The
consistency index tests whether redundancies are satis-
fied within expected experimental error. For chemostat
steady states of CHO cultures grown in a hydrolysate-
supplemented medium, consistent data were obtained
only when amino acids liberated from peptides were
taken into account. © 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. Biotech-
nol Bioeng 62: 324–335, 1999.
Keywords: peptide; serum-free media; metabolism; flux
analysis; material balance; continuous culture; chemo-
stat
INTRODUCTION
Mammalian cells can correctly fold and post-translationally
modify proteins in ways that simpler organisms cannot. Be-
cause of their ability to specifically process proteins, mam-
malian cells are used to produce many recombinant pro-
teins. Unfortunately, mammalian cells produce relatively
low quantities of recombinant protein, and the quality of the
product depends upon posttranslational processing. Improv-
ing product quantity and quality requires an understanding
of how recombinant protein production is related to cell
growth, maintenance, and metabolism. Therefore, metabolic
flux analysis provides a potentially useful tool for unravel-
ing how metabolic pathways influence recombinant protein
production.
A variety of techniques have been used to estimate intra-
cellular metabolic fluxes. Metabolite labeling techniques
using isotopic tracers detectable by either nuclear magnetic
reasonance (NMR) or radioactive decay can provide reliable
estimates for intracellular fluxes, but such methods are only
applicable for small-scale, targeted experiments (Fitzpatrick
et al., 1993; Kelleher, 1985; Mancuso et al., 1994; Sharf-
stein et al., 1994). Material balancing can also provide es-
timates for intracellular fluxes, given a metabolic network
stoichiometry and measurements of external metabolite
fluxes. When applied to complex networks, material bal-
ancing may not be sufficient to uniquely determine all
fluxes, because the number of unknown fluxes may exceed
the number of measurable metabolities. In these cases op-
timization criteria can be used to narrow the solution space,
or some of the fluxes may be measured directly (Bonarius et
al., 1996; Savinell and Palsson, 1992; Varma and Palsson,
1994). Alternatively the metabolic network can be simpli-
fied by lumping groups of serial reactions into single reac-
tions, and focusing on pathways that carry significant flux
(Xie and Wang, 1996; Zupke and Stephanopoulos, 1995).
Zupke and Stephanopoulos (1995) showed that metabolic
fluxes obtained by material balancing in this manner were
consistent with measured metabolite 13C NMR isotope dis-
tributions.
Applying material balancing techniques requires accurate
metabolite uptake and production data for all metabolities
with significant contribution to material balances. Potential
sources of metabolizable material in many serum-free me-
dia formulations are low molecular weight digests of bio-
* Present address: Genetics Institute, One Burtt Road, Andover, MA
01810
Correspondence to: Greg Stephanopoulos
Contract grant sponsors: National Science Foundation; National Insti-
tutes of Health
Contract grant numbers: EEC-9402065; 5T32GM08334
© 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
CCC 0006-3592/99/030324-12
 logical materials such as yeast extracts and plant or animal
tissue hydrolysates. These additives are commonly used to
improve growth in serum-free media (Bonarius, et al., 1996;
Keen and Rapson, 1995; Schumpp and Schlaeger, 1990;
Velez et al., 1986). Because of safety concerns related to the
use of animal-derived products in processes producing
biologics for human injection, plant-derived hydrolysates
are commonly favored over animal tissue hydrolysates
(Schumpp and Schlaeger, 1990). Regardless of the source
material, the major nutrients that hydrolysates provide in-
clude free amino acids, peptides, vitamins, minerals, and
undefined components. From a mass balancing viewpoint,
by far the most important compounds that hydrolysates sup-
ply are amino acids and peptides. The animal tissue digest
Primatone RL (Quest International, Norwich, NY), for ex-
ample, consists of 37% free amino acids and 41% pep-
tides by weight, with the majority of peptides in the 200 to
500 dalton (di- to tetrapeptide) size range (from manufac-
turer specifications). It is known that cultured animal cells
can metabolize dipeptides such as glycyl-L-glutamine and
L-alanyl-L-glutamine (Minamoto et al., 1991), and so one
would expect that other peptides could be utilized as well.
There is evidence that dipeptides and tripeptides are taken
up more readily in the intestine than corresponding free
amino acids, while tetra- and higher peptides are not readily
utilized (Grimble et al., 1987; Silk et al., 1985). While these
results are for specialized digestive tissue, the possibility
that cultured animal cell lines can also take up and utilize
peptide amino acids cannot be discounted. Thus, peptides
may supply a significant amount of amino acids to cells
grown in serum-free media formulations containing hydro-
lysates.
In this article we describe a method for quantifying the
consumption of amino acids from peptides in a complex
(hydrolysate-containing) medium. Chinese hamster ovary
cells producing recombinant human gamma interferon were
grown in continuous culture (chemostat) in a serum-free
medium which contained the animal tissue hydrolysate Pri-
matone RL. Amino acid uptake from peptides was moni-
tored, and metabolism was quantified using metabolite bal-
ancing. Data consistency analysis demonstrates that the
CHO cells did take up and utilize amino acids from pep-
tides, and this uptake must be quantified to properly account
for metabolite uptake and production. Consumption of pep-
tide amino acids should be considered whenever significant
amounts of peptides are present in the medium, for example
when plant or animal tissue hydrolysates are included in the
formulation.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Cell Line and Culture Medium
The Chinese hamster ovary cell line producing recombinant
human gamma interferon (g-CHO cells) was obtained from
Dr. Walter Fiers at the University of Ghent, Belgium (Sca-
hill et al., 1983). These cells were adapted to suspension
growth in a serum-free medium formation developed at
MIT. Designated RPMI-SFM, this medium uses RPMI-
1640 as the base and is supplemented with the following:
Primatone RL (2.5 g/L), 2-hydroxypropyl-b-cyclodextrin
(0.4 g/L), Pluronic F-68 (0.1% 4 1 g/L), insulin (5 mg/L),
transferrin (5 mg/L), trace elements (according to Hamilton
and Ham (1977)), EDTA (6.3 mg/L), sodium pyruvate (1
mM), putrescine (1 mM), choline chloride (11 mg/L), etha-
nolamine (100 mM), linoleic acid (1.5 mM), methotrexate
(0.25 mM), penicillin (100,000 units/L), and streptomycin
(100 mg/L). In batch culture, the cells have a doubling time
of ∼21 h and maximum cell densities of ∼2 × 106 cells/mL
when seeded at 2 × 105 cells/mL. For glucose-limited con-
tinuous culture, glucose-free RPMI-1640 was supplemented
with 0.5 g/L glucose (2.78 mM 4 25% of normal RPMI-
1640), and glutamine was added to a final concentration of
0.45 g/L (3.08 mM 4 150% of normal RPMI-1640). Pri-
matone RL was obtained from Quest International (Nor-
wich, NY) and all other components were obtained from
Sigma (St. Louis, MO).
Bioreactor Operation
Continuous cultures were carried out in a 2-L Applikon
(Foster City, CA) reactor with a 1.2-L working volume. A
heated water jacket maintained the temperature at 37°C, and
agitation was set at 200 rpm. The pH was controlled at 7.20
± .05 by addition upon demand of 320 mM HCl or a solu-
tion of 6.2 g/L NaOH and 0.4 g/L KOH. Carbon dioxide
was not included in the pH control loop so that the carbon
dioxide evolution rate could be more accurately measured
(see below). Oxygen was supplied via surface aeration by
mixing air with either nitrogen or oxygen so that the reactor
dissolved oxygen was maintained at 45% of air saturation,
with the gas flow rate controlled by a mass flow controller
at 35 mol/day (∼0.5 VVM). An ML-4100 multi-loop pro-
cessor controller (New Brunswick Scientific, Edison, NJ)
was used for the pH and oxygen control. Process data were
logged through the ML-4100 controller to a computer using
the AFS software from New Brunswick Scientific (Edison,
NJ).
Reactor seed cultures were grown in 250 mL spinner
flasks (Bellco, Vineland, NJ), and reactors were seeded at
approximately 4 × 105 cells/mL. After 24 h the feed and
waste pumps were started to initiate continuous culture.
Following five residence times, time invariance of the fol-
lowing variables was assumed to indicate a steady state:
total cell density, viability as measured by trypan blue ex-
clusion, glucose concentration, lactate concentration, and
oxygen uptake. When these parameters were steady within
a 10% range for 48 h, the system was assumed to be at
steady state.
Dry Cell Weight, Cell Number, and Viability
Cell number and viability were determined using a hema-
cytometer with Trypan blue exclusion. Dry cell weight was
determined by drying samples in a vacuum drying oven.
NYBERG ET AL.: PEPTIDE METABOLISM BY CHO CELLS

NYBERG ET AL.: PEPTIDE METABOLISM BY CHO CELLS
One hundred mL reactor samples were collected and cen-
trifuged at 250g for 10 min, and the supernatant was re-
moved. The cells were washed twice in phosphate buffered
saline and centrifuged. After carefully removing the super-
natant, cells were suspended in purified water and trans-
ferred to dry, pre-weighted aluminum weigh boats. Samples
were dried to a constant weight in a vacuum drying oven set
Because of extensive cell aggregation (clumping) at low
dilution rates, hemocytometer counts of cell number were
difficult, and dry cell weight was taken as a more accurate
measure of biomass production. The data presented in this
article are on a dry cell weight basis (a typical specific dry
cell weight for this cell line is 390 pg/cell).
The oxygen uptake rate (OUR) was obtained from the mass
balance for oxygen in the liquid phase of the reactor at
OUR = kLa ~C* −CR) + D~CF −CR!
where kLa ≡liquid phase mass transfer coefficient for oxy-
gen; C* ≡liquid phase concentration of oxygen at equilib-
rium with the gas phase; CR ≡concentration of oxygen in
the liquid phase of the reactor; D ≡dilution rate; CF ≡
concentration of oxygen in the liquid feed.
The first term on the right-hand side represents oxygen
transport across the gas–liquid interface. The second term
accounts for the net inflow of oxygen with the liquid
streams. Liquid concentrations of oxygen in the reactor and
the feed were measured with a blood gas analyzer (Ciba
Corning Diagnostics, Medfield, MA). The equilibrium
concentration, C*, was calculated based on the gas-phase
oxygen concentration at the exit of the reactor, which was
determined with a gas analyzer containing a paramag-
netic oxygen sensor (Columbus Instruments, Columbus,
OH). The partial pressures of oxygen, obtained from either
the blood gas analyzer or the gas analyzer, were converted
to liquid concentrations using Henry’s Law (CO2 4 PO2/
HO2). The Henry’s constant for our serum-free medium at
37°C was estimated as HO2 = 9.99 × 107 Pa*L/mol based
upon correlations presented in Schumpe et al. (1982) for
correcting gas solubilities to account for medium composi-
The carbon dioxide evolution rate (CER) was determined
from a mass balancing approach similar to that of Bonarius
et al. (1995), except that liquid-phase carbon dioxide levels
were measured rather than calculated. A mass balance for
carbon dioxide over the entire reactor at steady-state yields:
where n˙g ≡molar gas flowrate to the reactor; VR ≡liquid
CO2 ≡mole fraction of CO2 in re-
CO2 ≡mole fraction of CO2 in the feed
A ≡concentration of CO2 and bi-
carbonate in the liquid phase of the reactor; CF
tration of CO2 and bicarbonate in the liquid feed.
The first term on the right-hand side represents the net
inflow of CO2 between the entering and exiting gas streams.
The mole fractions of CO2 in gas streams were measured
with a gas analyzer containing an IR CO2 sensor (Columbus
Instruments, Columbus, OH). To allow for more accurate
CO2), CO2 was omitted from the feed gas
The second term on the right-hand side of Equation (2)
represents the net inflow of CO2 with the liquid streams. A
250-mL pre-mixing vessel was used to equilibrate the feed
liquid with air containing 12% CO2 to eliminate variation in
the feed CO2 levels due to loss of CO2 from the feed bottle.
The amount of CO2 in a particular liquid stream, present
mainly as dissolved CO2 and bicarbonate, was determined
by measuring the partial pressure of CO2 (PCO2) and the pH
using a blood gas analyzer (Ciba Corning Diagnostics,
Medfield, MA). The partial pressure of CO2 is related to the
dissolved CO2 concentration through Henry’s law. The bi-
carbonate concentration can then be determined by assum-
ing the simplified equilibrium relationship
which has an apparent equilibrium constant K84
(Putnam and Roos, 1991). Thus measuring the partial pres-
sure of CO2 and the pH allows the total dissolved carbon
dioxide plus bicarbonate concentration to be calculated
Based upon correlations in Schumpe et al. (1982), the Hen-
ry’s law constant for our serum-free medium at 37°C was
estimated as HCO2 4 4.20 × 106 Pa*L/mol, which is very
close to the value (4.19 × 106 Pa*L/mol) for cerebrospinal
fluid at 37°C and pH 4 7.20 (Severinghaus, 1965). Because
both the Henry’s law and equilibrium constants are func-
tions of ionic strength, one would expect aqueous solutions
with similar Henry’s constants to have similar equilibrium
constants. Therefore, we estimated the apparent equilibrium
constant as pK8 4 −log(K8) 4 6.138, which is the mea-
sured value for cerebrospinal fluid at 37°C and pH 4 7.20
Steady-state samples taken from the reactor were centri-
fuged at 200g for 8 min to remove the cells, and the super-
BIOTECHNOLOGY AND BIOENGINEERING, VOL. 62, NO. 3, FEBRUARY 5, 1999
natant was frozen at −20°C until ready for analysis. Three-
hundred and forty mL of thawed sample was deproteinated
before performing the lactate and glucose assays using 100
mL of 20% m/v trichloroacetic acid. Two hundred mL of
deproteinated sample was then neutralized with 50 mL of
25% m/v potassium bicarbonate. Enzymatic glucose and
lactate assays were performed according to the 16-UV and
826-UV Sigma assay protocols (Sigma, St. Louis, MO).
Samples for amino acid analysis were thawed and diluted
between 1.5 and 3.5 times, so that the maximum expected
amino acid concentration was less than 1 mM (the upper
limit of calibration). The internal standards norvaline and
sarcosine were added during dilution, so that they were both
present at a final concentration of 0.5 mM. Samples were
then deproteinated using Ultrafree-MC 5,000 MW cutoff
ultrafilters (Millipore, Bedford, MA). Amino acids were
analyzed using the AminoQuant protocol on an HP 1090
HPLC (Hewlett Packard, Palo Alto, CA). This protocol uses
pre-column derivitization with ortho-phthalaldehyde (OPA)
for detection of primary amino acids and 9-fluorenylmethyl
choloroformate (FMOC) for detection of secondary amino
acids (AminoQuant Series II Operator’s Handbook, Hewlett
Packard part #01090-90025). Separation was achieved on a
C18 reverse-phase column (Hewlett Packard, Palo Alto,
Gamma interferon concentrations were measured using a
commercially available ELISA kit (Endogen, Inc., Cam-
bridge, MA). Samples were diluted between 4500 and 6000
times in a 10 g/L solution of bovine serum albumin in
phosphate buffered saline (Pierce, Rockford, IL). The assay
was performed according to the manufacturer’s protocol.
Measuring Amino Acids in Peptides
Amino acids present in peptides can be quantified by first
deproteinating the sample and then acid hydrolyzing the
peptides into individual amino acids. By measuring the free
amino acid concentration before and after hydrolysis, the
amount of amino acids present in peptides can be calcu-
Peptide amino acids 4 (post-hydrolysis amino acids) −
The internal standards norvaline and sarcosine were
added to samples so that their final concentrations after
hydrolysis and neutralization would be 0.5 mM. Samples
were then deproteinated using pre-rinsed Ultrafree-MC
5,000 MW cutoff ultrafilters (Millipore, Bedford, MA). It
was important to pre-rinse the filters with water before use,
because the filters contain extractable triethylene glycol that
is present as a humectant. If this triethylene glycol is not
flushed from the membrane, then a brown precipitate will
form during the hydrolysis step. Following deproteination
the samples were dried in microcentrifuge tubes in a Speed-
Vac (Savant Instruments, Farmingdale, NY).
Samples were hydrolyzed using constant boiling (6 N)
hydrochloric acid according to the method of Chiou and
Wang (1989). Reusable Pyrex hydrolysis tubes as described
in Chiou and Wang (1988) were ordered from the Custom
Glass Shop, which is a division of the Kontes Glass Com-
pany (Vineland, NJ). Before each use the tubes were
cleaned with a 2:1 nitric to sulfuric acid mixture, and then
rinsed with purified water and dried. The deproteinated,
dried reactor samples were dissolved in 6 N HCl (Pierce,
Rockford, IL), and then 200 mL of each reconstituted
sample was added to separate hydrolysis tubes. The tubes
were purged of oxygen by alternately flowing nitrogen and
pulling a vacuum for 15-s intervals over 2 m while gently
agitating the tubes. The tubes were sealed while nitrogen
flowed over the openings. The samples were hydrolyzed
individually in a microwave at 80% of full power (approxi-
mately 1200 W) for 4 m each. Four minutes was established
as an appropriate time by hydrolyzing chicken egg white
lysozyme (Sigma, St. Louis, MO) for various times and
comparing actual amino acid recoveries to theoretical re-
covery based upon the amino acid sequence. Four minutes
provided good recovery for most amino acids, with less
destruction of labile amino acids such as serine (88% re-
covery) and threonine (91% recovery). Hydrolyzed samples
were cooled in ice water to condense volatilized HCl, and
then 100 mL of sample was neutralized with 75 mL of 8 M
NaOH and 25 mL of 0.4 N borate buffer. Amino acids were
then analyzed using the AminoQuant procedure described
above. Each sample was hydrolyzed in quadruplicate, with
amino acid measurements for each hydrolysis in duplicate.
The hydrolysis procedure described above causes exten-
sive degradation of tryptophan, and glutamine and aspara-
gine are deamidated to glutamate and aspartate, respec-
tively. Of these amino acids, glutamine and asparagine are
important in terms of central carbon metabolism. If peptides
containing the amino acids glutamate + glutamine (glx) or
aspartate + asparagine (asx) are consumed by the cells, then
the consumption of each of these individual amino acids
must be estimated. For the experiments described here,
there was no significant uptake of glutamine or glutamate
from peptides (i.e., glx measured in peptides was the same
in the feed and reactor samples within experimental error).
Significant peptide asx uptake was detected, however, and
so the asx was assumed to be 48% asparagine and 52%
aspartate, based upon a measured amino acid composition
for animal cell protein (Xie and Wang, 1994).
The simplified biochemical reaction network considered
here is depicted in Figure 1 and listed in Table I. The net-
work includes the reactions of central carbon metabolism,
which have been identified as important for producing bio-
mass and energy (Bonarius et al., 1996; Savinell and Pals-
son, 1992; Stryer, 1988; Xie and Wang, 1996; Zupke and
Stephanopoulos, 1995). Groups of serial reactions were
lumped into single reactions, and only pathways carrying
NYBERG ET AL.: PEPTIDE METABOLISM BY CHO CELLS
significant flux were included. In cases where recombinant
protein accounts for a significant fraction of total protein
synthesis, product formation should be included in the net-
work model as an additional reaction. In this case, produc-
tion of recombinant protein represented less than 0.4% of
biomass production, and so was neglected. The reactions
involved in central carbon metabolism are responsible for
satisfying the energy demands of the cell, as well as pro-
viding biosynthetic building blocks and generating major
waste products. Consumption of network intermediates for
biosynthesis was treated as described in Zupke and Stepha-
nopoulos (1995). The composition of CHO cells used for
deriving the biomass requirements was estimated from mea-
sured values for hybridoma cells (Xie and Wang, 1994).
The estimated biosynthesis demands are listed in Table II.
The amino acid requirements for protein synthesis were
estimated based upon the average amino acid composition
of proteins measured in a hybridoma cell extract (Xie and
Wang, 1994). This average amino acid composition agreed
well with an average amino acid frequency determined from
over 1000 proteins of known sequence (Creighton, 1993).
In this simplified network, flux through the pentose-
phosphate pathway is estimated from the biosynthetic de-
mand for ribose-5-P in nucleotide synthesis. There is no
accounting for recycling in the pentose-phosphate pathway
(PPP). NADPH production cannot unambiguously be used
to calculate the pentose phosphate recycle because of un-
certain transhydrogenase activity linking NADH and
NADPH, and the fact that the malic enzyme can use either
NAD+ or NADP+ as a cofactor (Eigenbrodt et al., 1985). In
this analysis we lump NADH and NADPH into a single
NAD(P)H pool of reducing power, and this NAD(P)H pool
is related to both biomass synthesis and oxygen uptake.
Under these assumptions, recycle in the pentose-phosphate
pathway and recycle in the citric acid (TCA) cycle are in-
distinguishable; in both cases two equivalents of reducing
power are generated per carbon dioxide evolved. The in-
ability to distinguish between TCA and PPP recycle makes
networks incorporating PPP recycle and lumped NAD(P)H
effectively singular. Thus, our estimate for the TCA cycle
flux may be an overestimate that also includes PPP recycle.
Letting xi represent the flux [mmole/(g DCW*d)] of reac-
tion i, ai,j represent the stoichiometric coefficient of me-
tabolite j in reaction i and rj represent the rate of accumu-
lation of metabolite j, a series of mass balances for each
metabolite can be written based upon the stoichiometry of
the reactions presented in Table I:
The resulting set of material balance equations can be ex-
where A is the matrix of stoichiometric coefficients, x is the
unknown vector of reaction fluxes and r is the vector of
metabolite production. The A matrix is determined by the
stoichiometry of the assumed biochemistry, and has dimen-
sions of m × n, where m is the number of metabolites in the
network and n is the number of reactions. The metabolite
production vector, r, consists of the actual measured pro-
duction rates for extracellular metabolites, while production
is assumed to be zero for intracellular metabolites based
upon the pseudo-steady-state (PSS) approximation. The
PSS assumption is considered reasonable, because the
fluxes consuming and producing internal metabolites are
much larger than their expected maximum rates of concen-
tration change (Zupke and Stephanopoulos, 1995).
The unknown fluxes can be determined from Equation
(5) using simple matrix inversion if A is square (m = n). In
general A will not be square, because it is desirable to
include redundant information so that the consistency of the
data and assumed biochemistry can be analyzed. In this case
m > n and the fluxes can be determined via the method of
weighted least squares (Strang, 1988):
where c is the variance-covariance matrix of the measure-
ment vector, r. Equation (6) can be used to give reliable
estimates for x assuming the matrix A is of full rank and is
reasonably well conditioned. A poorly conditioned matrix
will be very sensitive to slight changes in the measurements,
and hence will not give reliable flux estimates. A measure of
this sensitivity is given by the condition number, c(A), de-
where \ \ indicates a matrix norm. c(A) serves as an upper
bound to the relative error magnification inherent in solving
Ax 4 r. There are a number of ways for estimating the

but the two-norm (also called the spectral norm) provides
the smallest upper bound on the potential error magnifica-
tion (Gerald and Wheatley, 1994). The two-norm condition
number can be calculated from the singular value decom-

(Maron and Lopez, 1991). The system can be considered
well posed if c(A) is small (<100), while a large c(A)
(>1000) indicates that sensitivity problems exist. As men-
tioned above, our network including PPP recycle and
lumped NAD(P)H is effectively singular, because recycle in
the PPP and TCA cycle are indistinguishable. The two-
norm condition number for this network is 535,602, which
indicates that reliable estimates of PPP recycle are not pos-

Redundant data are necessary for testing the consistency of
measurements and assumed biochemistry. A systematic
method for analyzing data consistency was presented by
Romagnoli and Stephanopoulos (1981), and this approach
has been applied to fermentation data (Wang and Stepha-
nopoulos, 1983) and flux analysis (Vallino and Stepha-
nopoulos, 1990; Zupke and Stephanopoulos, 1995). The
method is based upon statistical hypothesis testing to deter-
mine whether redundancies are satisfied to within expected
experimental error. The reader is referred to the original
